---
title: "Methodology 3 — Homotopy in $\\delta$"
subtitle: "Why the final iterate lands at a vertex"
---

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
*Draft outline — content to come.*
:::

## The curvature parameter

$\delta$ in the relaxed objective controls how strongly the envelope penalty pushes $\boldsymbol t$ toward vertices. Small $\delta$ → smooth, easy to optimise. Large $\delta$ → strongly concave on the polytope.

## Homotopy schedule

Start with small $\delta$, increase geometrically:

$$
\delta^{(0)} < \delta^{(1)} < \cdots < \delta^{(R)} \;\ge\; \delta_{\mathrm{conc}}.
$$

Warm-start each FW solve with the previous $\boldsymbol t$.

## Theorem 1 — concavity threshold

There exists an explicit $\delta_{\mathrm{conc}}$ (depending on the design and the model) above which $f_{\delta, \lambda}(\boldsymbol t)$ is concave on the cardinality polytope, and **every minimiser is a binary vertex**.

For logistic regression:
$$
\delta_{\mathrm{conc}} \;=\; \frac{\nu_{\max}}{8n}.
$$

For multinomial:
$$
\delta_{\mathrm{conc}} \;=\; \frac{\nu_{\max}}{4n}.
$$

(where $\nu_{\max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of $X_u^\top X_u$.)

## The $k$-sweep

Loop over $k = 1, 2, \ldots, q$, warm-starting each $k$ from the previous solution. One sweep produces a full subset path $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_q$.

> Placeholder for visual: animation of the loss surface deforming with $\delta$ from smooth to spiky-at-corners; gif candidate.

## Where to next

See it work — in code, on real data:

- [Linear simulation](../demos/01-simulation.qmd)
- [HD logistic simulation](../demos/02-simulation-hd.qmd)
- [Khan SRBCT](../demos/03-khan.qmd)
- [Rice GWAS](../demos/04-rice.qmd)
- [Comparisons](../demos/05-comparisons.qmd)

---

[← Frank-Wolfe](02-frank-wolfe.qmd)
